figures.caption: Figure 4. Φ (τ = 50) in on- and off-periods (A) and their dynamics during the transitions (B). These Φ values were normalized to the mean value of the WAKE condition. Shaded areas represent the 95% confidence intervals. *** p < .001.

Figure 3. Relationships between Φ and firing rate per element in the V1 dataset. (A) Firing rate per element across conditions. (B) Relationship between firing rate per element and peak τ in the WAKE condition. (C) Relationship between firing rate per element and peak Φ. (D) Relationship between the changes in firing rate and peak Φ. (E) Firing rate per element after random down-sampling. (F) Φ in each condition after downsampling. Values in each session are normalized to the peak Φ of the WAKE condition as a baseline. (G) Relationship between the change in Φ and the effect size (Cohen's d of t-test per session) for NREM and WAKE conditions. A larger absolute effect size indicates a more stable difference between conditions. Solid lines indicate regression lines, and gray shaded areas denote the 95% confidence intervals. * p <.05, *** p < .001.

Figure 5. Φ changes in the FC dataset. (A) Individual Φ values as function of τ in the WAKE condition. (B) Distribution of peak τ in the WAKE condition of the FC dataset. (C) Number of neurons of the High τ and Low τ groups. (D) Firing rates per element of the High and Low τ groups. (E, G) Mean Φ as a function of τ across conditions for the Low τ (E) and High τ (G) groups. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean. (F, H) Normalized Φ values in each condition for the Low τ (F) and High τ (H) groups. (I) Firing rate per element across conditions. (I) Firing rates per element across conditions. (J) Relationship between the firing rate per element and the peak Φ value. (K) Relationship between the change in firing rate and the change in peak Φ (ΔΦNREM–WAKE and ΔΦREM–WAKE). Solid lines in the scatter plots indicate regression lines, and gray shaded areas denote the 95% confidence intervals. * p <.05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001.

Figure 2. Φ as a function of τ in layer-based or randomized systems for the V1 dataset. (A, C) Individual Φ values in the WAKE condition for layer-based (A) and randomized (C) systems. (B, D) Mean Φ across conditions for layer-based (B) and randomized (D) systems. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean. Gray shaded areas indicate τ values with a significant main effect of condition, as determined by independent ANOVAs (p < .05).

Figure 1. Analysis workflow. (A) Raster plot of a single session including WAKE, NREM, and REM epochs. (B) Frequency distribution of neurons by cortical depth. (C) Frequency distribution of neuron counts per cortical layer for each session. (D1&D2) Construction of layer-based and randomized systems. (E) Aggregation of neural activity into elements. (F) Binary signals were generated over discrete time using a window τ. The state sequence was obtained from the binary signals of the four selected elements. (G&H) The transition probability matrix (TPM) was computed from the state sequence, and Φ for each state was computed from the TPM. (I) The Φ value for each state was assigned to the corresponding state in the sequence, and the values were averaged in each condition.
